description: 20-question LightRAG QA benchmark generated from PDF content; every case must pass with deep_qa_mode on and off.

cases.question: Summarize the LightRAG paper in 5 bullet points.
evidence: LightRAG is a simple and fast retrieval-augmented generation framework that incorporates graph structures into text indexing and retrieval.
It uses dual-level retrieval over low-level entity details and high-level themes to answer detailed and abstract queries.
LightRAG supports incremental updates by integrating newly extracted entities and relationships into the existing graph without rebuilding the whole index.
The evaluation covers retrieval accuracy, model ablation, response efficiency, and adaptability to data changes.

cases.question: Compare LightRAG with GraphRAG.
evidence: GraphRAG is a graph-enhanced RAG system that uses an LLM to generate community reports to capture global information for high-level queries.
LightRAG uses graph-based text indexing plus low-level and high-level retrieval, combining specific entity exploration with broader topic retrieval.
While both LightRAG and GraphRAG use graph-based retrieval mechanisms, LightRAG consistently outperforms GraphRAG, particularly in larger datasets with complex language contexts.
In retrieval cost, GraphRAG traverses hundreds of communities, whereas LightRAG uses fewer than 100 tokens for keyword generation and retrieval and requires one API call.

cases.question: Performance of ablated versions of LightRAG. Please include a comparison table.
evidence: Table 2: Performance of ablated versions of LightRAG, using NaiveRAG as reference. | Variant | Key interpretation | Example LightRAG-side results | | Full LightRAG | hybrid low-level and high-level retrieval | Agriculture Overall 67.6%, Legal Overall 84.8% | | -High | removes high-order retrieval | Agriculture Overall 64.8%, Legal Overall 78.0% | | -Low | removes low-level retrieval | Agriculture Overall 65.2%, Legal Overall 81.2% | | -Origin | removes original text | Agriculture Overall 74.4%, Legal Overall 84.4% |
The -High variant removes high-order retrieval and leads to performance decline across nearly all datasets and metrics.
The -Low variant uses high-level retrieval and can improve comprehensiveness, but may lose depth for precise detailed answers.
The full LightRAG hybrid mode combines low-level and high-level retrieval and achieves balanced performance across multiple dimensions.

cases.question: Reproduce the Table 1 GraphRAG vs LightRAG win rates for Legal and Mix as a comparison table.
evidence: Table 1 GraphRAG vs LightRAG win rates. | Dataset | Metric | GraphRAG | LightRAG | | Legal | Comprehensiveness | 48.4% | 51.6% | | Legal | Diversity | 26.4% | 73.6% | | Legal | Empowerment | 43.6% | 56.4% | | Legal | Overall | 47.2% | 52.8% | | Mix | Comprehensiveness | 50.4% | 49.6% | | Mix | Diversity | 36.0% | 64.0% | | Mix | Empowerment | 50.8% | 49.2% | | Mix | Overall | 50.4% | 49.6% |

cases.question: What does Figure 1 show about the LightRAG architecture?
evidence: Figure 1: Overall architecture of the proposed LightRAG framework.
The architecture extracts entities and relationships, builds a graph-based text index, and uses low-level and high-level key-value retrieval for answer generation.

cases.question: Explain Figure 2 and the cost comparison between GraphRAG and LightRAG.
evidence: Figure 2 compares cost in terms of total tokens and API calls for GraphRAG and LightRAG on the Legal dataset.
In retrieval, GraphRAG uses 610 level-2 community reports, each averaging 1,000 tokens, totaling 610,000 tokens.
LightRAG uses fewer than 100 tokens for keyword generation and retrieval and requires only a single API call.

cases.question: What is the formula-like cost expression for GraphRAG incremental update, and what does it mean?
evidence: For incremental updates, GraphRAG would require around 1,399 × 2 × 5,000 tokens to reconstruct both original and new community reports.
The expression uses 1,399 communities, two sets of community reports, and roughly 5,000 tokens per community report, showing high update cost.

cases.question: What is the difference between low-level and high-level retrieval in LightRAG?
evidence: Low-level retrieval focuses on specific entities and their immediate neighbors for detailed information.
High-level retrieval captures broader topics and entity-wise relationships to gather more extensive and varied information.
LightRAG combines low-level and high-level retrieval in hybrid mode to provide both breadth and depth.

cases.question: List the four research questions RQ1 to RQ4 in the evaluation section.
evidence: RQ1 asks how LightRAG compares to existing RAG baseline methods in generation performance.
RQ2 asks how dual-level retrieval and graph-based indexing enhance generation quality.
RQ3 asks what advantages LightRAG demonstrates through case examples.
RQ4 asks about the costs associated with LightRAG and its adaptability to data changes.

cases.question: Which baselines are compared against LightRAG, and where does GraphRAG fit?
evidence: The baselines include NaiveRAG, RQ-RAG, HyDE, and GraphRAG.
GraphRAG is described as a graph-enhanced RAG system that uses an LLM to generate community reports for global information.

cases.question: What does Table 3 show in the case study comparing GraphRAG and LightRAG?
evidence: Table 3: Case Study: Comparison Between LightRAG and the Baseline Method GraphRAG.
The query asks which methods can normalize feature values for effective machine learning.
The LLM judge selects Answer 2 (LightRAG) for comprehensiveness, diversity, empowerment, and overall winner.
LightRAG covers a broader array of metrics including MAPK, AUC, user engagement metrics, precision, recall, RMSE, and F-Measure.

cases.question: Why is the Legal dataset important in the paper's comparison?
evidence: The Legal dataset is the largest dataset and contains millions of tokens.
The paper states that LightRAG's advantage over GraphRAG is particularly clear in larger datasets with complex language contexts such as Legal.
For Legal diversity in Table 1, GraphRAG has 26.4% while LightRAG has 73.6%.

cases.question: How does LightRAG handle incremental updates compared with GraphRAG?
evidence: LightRAG integrates newly extracted entities and relationships into the existing graph without full reconstruction.
GraphRAG must dismantle its existing community structure and regenerate community reports when new data is added.
This makes LightRAG more efficient and cost-effective for dynamic updates.

cases.question: Create a comparison table of LightRAG, GraphRAG, and NaiveRAG based on retrieval strategy and cost.
evidence: | Method | Retrieval strategy | Cost / limitation | | LightRAG | graph-based text index with low-level and high-level retrieval | fewer than 100 retrieval tokens and one API call in Legal retrieval | | GraphRAG | community reports and graph-enhanced retrieval | 610,000 retrieval tokens and many API calls on Legal | | NaiveRAG | top-k chunk retrieval baseline | lacks relational graph structure and loses on large corpora |

cases.question: From Table 2, which ablated or full variant performs best on Legal Overall, and what are the numbers?
evidence: Table 2 Legal Overall results: Full LightRAG 84.8%, -High 78.0%, -Low 81.2%, and -Origin 84.4%.
Thus the full LightRAG variant is the best on Legal Overall, narrowly ahead of -Origin.

cases.question: What does the paper say about LightRAG's diversity advantage?
evidence: LightRAG demonstrates a significant advantage in the Diversity metric, particularly within the larger Legal dataset.
In Table 1 GraphRAG versus LightRAG on Legal Diversity, GraphRAG is 26.4% and LightRAG is 73.6%.
The paper attributes this to the dual-level retrieval paradigm that retrieves from both low-level and high-level dimensions.

cases.question: Why does graph-based text indexing help LightRAG?
evidence: Graph-based text indexing extracts entities and relationships from chunks and builds graph structures.
The constructed graph structures enable extraction of global information from multi-hop subgraphs.
This enhances LightRAG's ability to handle complex queries spanning multiple document chunks.

cases.question: Reproduce a compact comparison table for NaiveRAG versus LightRAG on Agriculture Overall and Legal Overall.
evidence: | Dataset | Metric | NaiveRAG | LightRAG | | Agriculture | Overall | 32.4% | 67.6% | | Legal | Overall | 15.2% | 84.8% |

cases.question: 結果來看哪個比較好？

history.content: 比較這篇 document 不同方法的 performance.
The methods compared include LightRAG, GraphRAG, NaiveRAG and ablated LightRAG variants.

evidence: Across Table 1 and the case study, LightRAG generally performs best, outperforming GraphRAG especially on larger datasets and diversity metrics.
For Legal Diversity, GraphRAG is 26.4% while LightRAG is 73.6%; Table 3 also selects LightRAG as the overall winner over GraphRAG.

cases.question: What trade-offs or limitations are implied by the ablation and cost analyses?
evidence: The -High and -Low ablations show a trade-off between detailed entity exploration and broader topic coverage.
Low-level-only retrieval can be too narrow for complex comprehensive queries, while high-level-only retrieval can lose depth for precise detailed answers.
The cost analysis implies GraphRAG has high retrieval and update overhead, while LightRAG reduces retrieval cost with compact keyword generation and incremental graph updates.
